raw_recent_text: "Ugh, not again," you spout as the old wagon shutters, then dies.
Stranded on the road again, but this time, your money has gone dry.
All but your 50 dollar bill that Grandma gave you on her last Christmas.
You've been saving it for when you needed it most, but surely, it won't be enough.
The door creaks open, and you step out into the night sky, coughing up the smoke from your fried vehicle.
After pushing your car off the road and between the trees, there isn't much else left for you to do,
so you begin to wander down the dark, lonely street.
But at the end of the road, where concrete turned to stone turned to dirt, you notice a light up ahead, on the top of a hill.
As you waltz into the old, wooden shack, your eyes begin to light up with the fire of a thousand suns.
Roulette wheels! Poker tables! And in a dark corner of the abandoned casino, sits a dealer, shuffling cards for a new round of Blackjack.
That 50 dollars might just come in handy after all. Thanks, Grandma!
As you go to sit down at the table, you hear the Dealer cough, then watch as he sits up.
In a deep, and yet strained voice, the Dealer, cloaked in darkness, poses a question to you.
Would you like to play a game of Blackjack?
You have $50

A bird is singing outside your car. You don't want to punch it. Progress.
You hear it before you see it through your windshield. Quacking. So much quacking.
Around the corner comes a parade of ducks. At least thirty of them, waddling in perfect formation.
The lead duck - wearing what appears to be a tiny top hat - stops in front of you and quacks authoritatively.
You fall in line behind the ducks. People stare. You don't care.
The parade leads you through alleys, across a parking lot, and finally to... a fountain full of coins.
The top hat duck looks at you, then at the fountain, then quacks approvingly.
You fish out $16 in loose change. The ducks quack in celebration.
Your new balance is $189 + $16 = $205
Whiskers is already awake, sitting perfectly still on the dashboard, silhouetted against the sunrise. She looks regal.
Having Whiskers by your side makes the world a little less cruel.
The dream is about water. Rising water. You're in the car and it's filling up. First your feet. Then your lap. Then your chest.
You can't open the doors. The windows are locked. The water is warm, which is somehow worse.
You gasp awake. Bone dry. Heart hammering. You open the car door just to prove you can.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you walk to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
You look... determined. Or desperate. Hard to tell.

and an old man with tattoo sleeves and long black hair steps out.
He kicks his car, and the engine starts blaring once more.
"Hello, the name's Frank. Now I've got a baseball game to catch, but it looks like you could use some help."
Frank pulls a shiny silver hammer out of his pocket, and walks to the hood of your beaten down wagon.
"My god. This is just awful. Tell you what, I can fix this up for like 100 bucks,
and your engine will be runnin' just as good as mine. You game?"
"What?! How could you not accept my service? I'm the cheapest damn autoshop worker on this here planet!
But NOOOO, NOT FRANK! Never Frank. He Voted For Trump!
Let's all ridicule frank for his political party. You god damn liberals."
Frank spits in your face, and get back in his truck.
You watch as he revs his engine, gets out of his truck, kicks his beater, gets back in, revs his engine, and speeds off into the horizon.
Whiskers demands breakfast by walking across your face. Repeatedly.

Bored out of your mind, you decide to wander along the side of the road, just to get a change of scenery from the dusty leather seats of your wagon.

"In the grand tapestry of existence, one must consider the intricate dance of the cosmos, where each celestial body moves in perfect harmony, except when they don't, which is most of the time, really, leading one to ponder whether the stars are just freestyling it. This brings to mind the importance of breakfast, the most important meal of a day that is itself a construct, much like the notion that socks should always match or that cats have any respect for personal space, which is to say, it's all a matter of perspective, isn't it? That was some quality sleep. Five stars. Would sleep again.
A blaring engine roars down the road towards you.
As you scratch your eyes awake, you read 'Tom's Trusty Trucks and Tires' painted on the hood of a bright gold truck.
Waving the vehicle down, the truck slows, then halts, and an old, jolly man jumps out.
"Well, howdy! The name's Tom. It appears you've gotten yourself in a bit of a pickle, ya think?"
Tom pulls a big red wrench out of his pocket, and walks to the hood of your beaten down wagon.
"Yep, this thing's busted alright! Tell ya what, for, I don't know, 200 bucks,
I'll get this thing replaced for ya, good as new! Whaddya say?"
"Really? Awesome! I'll be the best dang mechanic this ol' automobile has ever seen!"
You watch in awe, as Tom, a man who has clearly perfected his craft, fixes up your wagon in no time. Sweet.
Your new balance is $468 - $200 = $268
Your car has been fixed! You can now drive around!
"Well, gee, this has been fun. Be seein' you around, ya know?"
And with that, you watch as his big golden truck stutters, starts, then drives away.
Whiskers has been scratching the upholstery. Passive-aggressive redecorating.

You wake up to scratching noises. Something is ON your car.
You look up. A family of raccoons is making themselves at home on your roof.
Mom raccoon. Dad raccoon. Three baby raccoons. All staring at you through the windshield with those little bandit masks.
You honk the horn. They don't flinch. You honk again. The babies yawn.
You stare at each other for a full minute. They're not leaving. This is their car now.
You go back to sleep. In the morning, they've left a dead mouse on your hood. A gift? A threat? Both?

Night falls. The game begins.

At a gas station counter, you spot a black matchbook mixed in with the free local church flyers.
Stamped on the cover in curling green ink: The Witch Doctor's Tower
Inside is a hand-drawn route through the marsh and a note that reads, "Bring cash, blood, or a good story."
The cashier sees you reading it and mutters that the old woman out there fixes things hospitals can't.

Whiskers knocks your coffee off the dashboard. On purpose. While maintaining eye contact.

You get in your car and drive to the Doctor's Office.
As you pull up closer to the bright blue building, you notice that the parking lot is concerningly empty.
You park your wagon right up front next to the entrance, and step out towards the doors.
When you enter into the lobby, you're immediately hit with the strong smell of hand sanitizer in the air.
The carpets are dull and brown, the light above you is flickering,
and the walls are filled with posters telling you to 'Floss More Often!' and 'Wash Your Hands Before You Eat!'
If you didn't know any better, you would have guessed you were on a movie set.
Walking towards the front desk, you see a cheery old lady, who looks up from her computer to smile at you.
Her gray hair covers her glasses, and her hand trembles as she hands you a pen and a clipboard with some paperwork.
Of course it's paperwork.
After filling out your information, you walk back to the front desk, and hand the lady the clipboard. She smiles, and begins to speak to you.
I see you're here for a checkup. The Doctor will see you now.
Hey there champ! How are you? Doing all right? Let's check you out and make sure you're all up to snuff.
Why, you look just as healthy as the day I met you, fresh from your mother's womb! Let me just give you this lollipop and you'll be free to go.
You walk back to the front desk to checkout.
That will be $146

You lean against the hood of the wagon and look up. You find Orion's Belt. The Big Dipper. That's... all you know.

Still, it's beautiful. You feel small, but in a comforting way.

He glances around nervously, then leans closer.
"Name's Gus. Grimy Gus, they call me. Got a little shop down on Fifth and Nowhere. "
"If you ever find yourself with... unusual items... things that don't belong in the light of day... I can make them disappear. For a fair price."
He taps his nose conspiratorially.
"Collectibles, trinkets, treasures. Things you picked up on your... adventures. I don't ask questions. Just cash on the barrel."
He hands you a grimy business card through the crack in the window. It reads: "Grimy Gus's Pawn Emporium - We Buy What Others Won't"
"Come by sometime. You won't regret it. Probably."
He shuffles off into the morning mist before you can respond.

You drive your car to a gas station for supplies. Just snacks. Maybe some coffee.
You're browsing the chips when the door slams open.
"EVERYBODY ON THE GROUND! NOW!"
A man with a shotgun. Ski mask. Shaking hands. This is really happening.
The cashier freezes. A mother clutches her child. An old man drops his coffee.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
The robber swings the gun around, wild-eyed.
You duck behind a shelf. Slowly, carefully, you crawl toward the back.
The robber is focused on the register. You're almost to the storage room...
A chip bag crinkles under your knee. The robber spins.
"I SEE YOU! GET OUT HERE!"
The shotgun is pointed right at you. You raise your hands and step out.
"Think you're smart? Think you can hide from me?"
He hits you with the stock. Your vision explodes into stars.
Your mind feels... foggy. Something isn't right.

Instinct screams at you: GET OUT. GET OUT NOW.
You throw yourself out of the car and run. Don't look back. Just run.
You're ten feet away when the engine catches fire. Fifteen when it explodes.
The shockwave knocks you flat. Heat washes over you. Debris rains down.
Your car—your home—is a fireball. Everything you owned is gone.
But you're alive. Somehow. Alive.
The edges of your vision blur. Reality feels thin.
Your reflection in the rearview mirror blinks before you do.
You have a nice dream for once. About the life you used to have.
A kitchen. Morning sunlight. Coffee in a real mug, not a gas station cup. Someone calls your name from another room.
Waking up is hard. The contrast between the dream and the dashboard is a knife in the chest.

your current sanity: 31%
your neck doesn't hurt. your back doesn't hurt. nothing hurts. is this a trap?
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $900, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"jolly good! stuart!"
you watch as the little man walks to the front of your wagon, opens the hood, and jumps in.
you can't really see what's going on, but after a couple of minutes, stuart jumps back out, covered in oil.
your new balance is $1,661 - $900 = $761
your car has been fixed! you can now drive around!
"oh my stuart! someone got a little too excited, didn't you?
yep, you're getting a bath as soon as we get back to the shop.
thanks again, stranger, it's been a pleasure doing business with you.
i recall it's good custom to tip after events like this, yes? here, take this."
oswald hands you a bright green bill, worth $100.
your new balance is $761 + $100 = $861
and with that, you watch as stuart rolls up the red carpet. oswald and stuart get back in the limo, and drive off into the distance.

your current sanity: 31%
your neck doesn't hurt. your back doesn't hurt. nothing hurts. is this a trap?
a glossy black limousine is quietly approaching your wagon.
as you sit up from your slumber, you read 'oswald's optimal outoparts' cursively engraved in gold letters on the side of the limo.
waving the vehicle down, the limo slows, then stops before you.
the door opens vertically, and a large red carpet is rolled out onto the street.
you watch in awe as a man, with a combover and a tuxedo, walks out before you. he coughs, then speaks.
"why hello there! the name's oswald, as you can see by my nametag.
do you like my bowtie? well of course you do! it appears your limousine has broken down."
oswald pulls a gold whistle out of his pocket, and blows into it deeply.
"oh stuart!" you watch as a bald man in a tailcoat suit, no taller than 4 feet, hobbles over to oswald's side.
"this is stuart! he will fix your limousine up for a fair price.
let's say, i don't know, i suppose a fair price is 500,000 dollars.
okay, the look on your face says that i'm making a big mistake.
let's try $900, and stuart here will get you back on the road! do you accept?"
"jolly good! stuart!"
you watch as the little man walks to the front of your wagon, opens the hood, and jumps in.
you can't really see what's going on, but after a couple of minutes, stuart jumps back out, covered in oil.
your new balance is $1,661 - $900 = $761
your car has been fixed! you can now drive around!
"oh my stuart! someone got a little too excited, didn't you?
yep, you're getting a bath as soon as we get back to the shop.
thanks again, stranger, it's been a pleasure doing business with you.
i recall it's good custom to tip after events like this, yes? here, take this."
oswald hands you a bright green bill, worth $100.
your new balance is $761 + $100 = $861
and with that, you watch as stuart rolls up the red carpet. oswald and stuart get back in the limo, and drive off into the distance.

For a moment, you could have sworn the shadows moved.
As you're walking along the side of the road, something catches your eye. A flash of white, darting between the bushes. A rabbit!
Without thinking, you give chase. The little creature bounds ahead of you, zigging and zagging through the underbrush with seemingly effortless grace.
You run and run, but no matter how fast you go, the rabbit stays just out of reach. Its white tail bobs mockingly in the moonlight.
Finally, you stop, hands on your knees, gasping for breath. When you look up, the rabbit is gone, vanished into the night like it was never there.
You trudge back to your wagon, defeated. But something tells you this isn't the last time you'll see that rabbit.
As the sun begins to set, and the stars light up in the night sky, you drive over to the casino, eager to play more Blackjack.
The neon signs blur together. For a moment, you can't remember why you came here.
Back for more? The cards appreciate your persistence.
The Dealer looks at you strangely. His jade eye narrows.
He does NOT seem pleased to see you tonight.
You have $861

It's a photograph. Old. Faded. Wallet-sized.

standing in front of a yellow house. The woman is laughing. The girl is blowing out candles
on a birthday cake.
The Dealer tucks the photo into his vest pocket without a word. His hands,
always perfectly steady, have the faintest tremor.
The Dealer tucks the photo into his vest pocket without a word. His hands,
always perfectly steady, have the faintest tremor.
The Dealer stops shuffling. He looks at you with those dark eyes.
For a moment, he's not the Dealer. He's just a man with a memory that hurts.
"Some cards aren't meant to be played. Let's leave it at that."
He resumes shuffling. The moment is over. But the tremor isn't.
For the rest of the session, the Dealer plays differently. Quieter.

Your current sanity: 26%
Your eyes pop open and you're ready to go. Did someone slip you a good dream? Because that was chef's kiss.
You find yourself on a bridge. You don't remember leaving your car.
You're standing at the railing. Looking down at the water. So far down.
Your hands are on the cold metal. When did you get out of the car?
The water looks peaceful. Dark and cold and peaceful. No more debt. No more hunger.
No more losing. No more trying. Just... nothing.
A voice in your head says: It would be so easy. Just let go.
No more losing. No more trying. Just... nothing.
A voice in your head says: It would be so easy. Just let go.
You stay at the railing. Minutes pass. Maybe hours.
A car stops. Someone gets out. A woman's voice, soft and scared.
"Hey. Hey there. Are you okay? Please step back from the edge."
You look at her. She looks terrified. Terrified for YOU.
When's the last time anyone was scared for you? When's the last time anyone cared?
You step back. She holds you while you cry. A stranger. Holding you while you fall apart.

You slept like a baby. A big, grown baby in a car, but a baby nonetheless.
You took a wrong turn. Or maybe the road took it for you.
You end up at a cemetery. Old one. Iron fence, stone angels, headstones so weathered
the names have worn to whispers.
Your car dies at the gate. Engine just... stops. You turn the key. Nothing.
You're stuck here until it decides to work again.
The gate swings open with a groan that echoes. Inside, the graves stretch out in rows,
ordered and patient, like they've been waiting.
Some have fresh flowers. Most don't. You read names as you walk:
"Harold Mercer, 1942-2019. Beloved husband."
"Elaine Frye, 1955-2021. She sang every morning."
"Baby James, 2008-2008. Too good for this world."
That last one stops you. You stand there for a while.
You hear digging. Somewhere deeper in the cemetery, someone is working.

"Welcome, welcome, welcome to Oswald's Optimal Outoparts! "

"Of course, he wouldn't be where he is today without my business acumen. My entrepreneurial spirit! My VISION!"
Oswald gestures grandly around the shop.
"You see, I'm not just a mechanic. I'm a businessman! An investor! "
"I see opportunity where others see obstacles. "
"Why, just last week I was thinking about expanding into the entertainment industry. "
"Casinos! Gambling! There's SO much money to be made!"
